---
title: "2.1.1 Data packets"
subtitle: "Data Transmission P45-P49"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        <style>
        .reveal .term { color: #cf1f2e; font-weight: 700; }
        .reveal .mark { background: #fff36d; padding: 0 0.08em; }
        .reveal .tight-list li { margin-bottom: 0.24em; }
        .reveal .clean-table table { font-size: 0.76em; line-height: 1.1; }
        .reveal .clean-table th, .reveal .clean-table td { padding: 0.24em 0.32em; vertical-align: top; }
        </style>
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the main outline

[← Back to Unit 2 Directory / 返回 Unit 2 目录](../../index.html)

## Data Packets

### Breaking data into chunks

Data sent over long distances is usually broken up into <span class="term">data packets</span> (数据包), sometimes called <span class="term">datagrams</span>:

::: {.tight-list}
- Large data broken into <span class="mark">small chunks (typically 64 KiB)</span>
- Much easier to control than a long continuous stream of data
- Each packet can be sent along a <span class="mark">different route</span> to its destination
- Great benefit if a particular transmission route is <span class="mark">out of action or very busy</span>
- The only obvious drawback is the <span class="mark">need to reassemble the data</span> when it reaches its destination
:::

## Packet Structure

### Header, payload, and trailer

A typical packet is split up into three parts:

::: {.tight-list}
**Packet header:**
- <span class="mark">IP address of the sending device</span>
- <span class="mark">IP address of the receiving device</span>
- <span class="mark">Sequence number</span> of the packet (allows correct reassembly)
- <span class="mark">Packet size</span> (to check if all packets have arrived intact)
- Note: header often also contains the total number of packets in this transmission

**Payload:**
- The <span class="mark">actual data</span> being sent in the packet (usually about 64 KiB)

**Packet trailer:**
- A way of <span class="mark">identifying the end of the packet</span> — essential to separate packets as they travel
- An <span class="term">error checking method</span>: Cyclic Redundancy Checks (CRC) are used
:::

## CRC Error Checking

### Cyclic Redundancy Check in packet trailer

<span class="term">CRC (Cyclic Redundancy Check)</span> (循环冗余校验) checks data packets:

::: {.tight-list}
1. The sending computer <span class="mark">adds up all the 1-bits in the payload</span>
2. This total is stored as a <span class="mark">hex value</span> in the trailer before sending
3. Once the packet arrives, the receiving computer <span class="mark">recalculates the number of 1-bits</span> in the payload
4. The computer then checks this value against the one sent in the trailer
5. If the two values <span class="mark">match</span>, no transmission errors have occurred
6. If they <span class="mark">do not match</span>, the packet needs to be <span class="mark">re-sent</span>
:::

## Packet Switching

### Routing through networks

<span class="term">Packet switching</span> (分组交换) is a method of data transmission in which a message is broken up into a number of packets. Each packet can then be sent <span class="mark">independently</span> from start point to end point.

At each stage in the transmission, there are nodes that contain a <span class="term">router</span> (路由器). Each router:
- Receives a data packet and, based on the information in the header, <span class="mark">decides where to send it next</span>
- Determines which route the packet needs to take, using the <span class="mark">destination IP address</span>
- Routing selection depends on the <span class="mark">number of packets waiting to be processed</span> at each node
- The <span class="mark">shortest possible path available</span> is always selected — this may not always be the shortest possible path, since certain parts of the route may be too busy or not suitable

::: {.tight-list}
- Packets can reach the destination <span class="mark">in a different order</span> to that in which they were sent
- At the destination, packets will need to be <span class="mark">reassembled into their correct order</span> (using the sequence number sent in the header)
:::

## Benefits of Packet Switching

### Advantages of this approach

::: {.tight-list}
- <span class="mark">No need to tie up a single communication line</span>
- <span class="mark">Overcome failed, busy or faulty lines</span> by simply re-routing packets
- <span class="mark">Relatively easy to expand</span> package usage
- <span class="mark">High data transmission rate</span> is possible
:::

## Drawbacks of Packet Switching

### Limitations to consider

::: {.tight-list}
- <span class="mark">Packets can be lost</span> and need to be re-sent
- The method is <span class="mark">more prone to errors with real-time streaming</span> (e.g. a live sporting event transmitted over the internet)
- There is a <span class="mark">delay at the destination</span> whilst the packets are being re-ordered
:::

## Router Role in Packet Switching

### Moving packets between networks

<span class="term">Routers</span> (路由器) make packet switching possible:

::: {.tight-list}
- <span class="mark">Examine destination IP</span> in each packet's header
- <span class="mark">Consult routing decisions</span> to find next node
- <span class="mark">Forward packet</span> to the next appropriate router
- <span class="mark">Make independent decisions</span> for each packet
- Thousands of routers interconnected across the internet
- Packets hop from router to router (node to node) until reaching destination
:::

## Hopping: Preventing Lost Packets

### How packets that never arrive are handled

Sometimes packets get lost because they keep <span class="mark">'bouncing' around from router to router</span> and never actually reach their destination. This would eventually clog the network.

To overcome this, a method called <span class="term">hopping</span> (跳数) is used:

::: {.tight-list}
- A <span class="mark">hop number</span> is added to the header of each packet
- This number is <span class="mark">reduced by 1</span> every time the packet leaves a router
- Each packet has a <span class="mark">maximum hop number</span> to start with
- Once a hop number reaches <span class="mark">zero</span>, the packet is <span class="mark">deleted</span> when it reaches the next router
- The missing packets will then be flagged by the receiving computer
- A <span class="mark">request to re-send</span> these packets will be made
:::

Note: hopping is not included on the syllabus but helps understand how packets can get lost.

## Packet Reassembly

### Reconstructing data at destination

Destination must rebuild original data:

::: {.tight-list}
- Packets can reach the destination <span class="mark">in a different order</span> to that in which they were sent
- <span class="mark">Sequence numbers in the header</span> identify the correct packet order
- <span class="mark">Missing packets detected</span> (gaps in sequence numbers)
- Reassembled into original order using sequence information
- The receiving computer checks packet sizes to ensure all packets are intact
- Transparent to user — happens automatically
:::

## Classroom Check

### Understand packets and packet switching

A complete answer should include:

::: {.tight-list}
- <span class="mark">packets are small chunks (typically 64 KiB)</span> — easier to control than continuous streams
- packet header contains <span class="mark">sender/receiver IP address, sequence number, packet size</span>
- packet trailer contains <span class="mark">end-of-packet identifier and CRC error check</span>
- <span class="mark">CRC: sending computer adds up all 1-bits in payload</span>, receiver recalculates and compares
- <span class="mark">packet switching routes each packet independently</span>; routers decide next hop by destination IP
- benefits: <span class="mark">no single line tied up, overcome failed lines, easy to expand, high data rate</span>
- drawbacks: <span class="mark">packets can be lost, errors with real-time streaming, delay during re-ordering</span>
- <span class="mark">hopping: hop number prevents lost packets clogging the network</span>
:::

## Bridge

### Next: Data Transmission Methods

The next deck covers serial, parallel, and duplex transmission methods.

## End

### Return to the main outline

[← Back to Unit 2 Directory / 返回 Unit 2 目录](../../index.html)
